intro.title: How to Use This Worksheet
intro.body: This worksheet covers everything you need for the Module 1 Exam—plus additional Security+ concepts that will serve you well beyond this course. If you work through this honestly, you’ll be prepared. If you skip it, you’re gambling. Your call.

content.text: Define each term. If you can’t explain it simply, you don’t understand it well enough.

items: Confidentiality
Integrity
Availability
Non-repudiation (the unofficial fourth pillar)
Threat
Vulnerability
Risk
Exploit
Zero-day attack
Attack surface
Attack vector vs. threat vector
Indicators of Compromise (IoCs)
Authentication — proving who you are
Authorization — what you’re allowed to do
Accounting — logging what you did
Script kiddie — motivation, capability, typical attacks
Hacktivist — motivation, typical targets
Organized crime — motivation, business model
Nation-state actor / Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) — characteristics, dwell time
Insider threat — why it’s harder to detect than external threats
Competitor — corporate espionage motivations
White hat — authorized, ethical
Black hat — malicious, unauthorized
Gray hat — unauthorized but not malicious; ethical ambiguity
Bug bounty programs — how they fit in
Responsible disclosure vs. full disclosure
Target of opportunity vs. targeted attack — what’s the difference?
Active attack vs. passive attack
Insider attack vs. outsider attack
Virus — requires host file, user action to spread
Worm — self-replicating, no user action needed
Trojan — disguised as legitimate software
Ransomware — encryption, extortion, why modern versions are hard to reverse
Rootkit — hides at OS/kernel level
Spyware — monitors user activity
Keylogger — captures keystrokes
RAT (Remote Access Trojan) — persistent backdoor
Botnet — network of compromised systems
Logic bomb — triggered by condition
Fileless malware — lives in memory, no files on disk

content.label: Key question:
content.text: What is the PRIMARY difference between a virus and a worm?

items: Phishing — mass email, generic targets
Spear phishing — targeted at specific individual
Whaling — targeting executives
Business Email Compromise (BEC) — impersonating CEO, wire fraud
Vishing — voice phishing (phone calls)
Smishing — SMS phishing
Pretexting — fabricated scenario to extract info
Baiting — leaving infected USB drives
Tailgating / piggybacking — following through secure doors
Shoulder surfing — watching someone enter credentials
Dumpster diving — searching trash for sensitive info
Watering hole attack — compromising sites your target visits

content.text: These aren’t just definitions — understand WHY each principle matters.

items: Least privilege — minimum access necessary
Separation of duties — no single person controls entire process
Defense in depth / layered security — multiple overlapping controls
Need to know — access based on job requirements
Implicit deny / default deny — if not explicitly allowed, it’s denied
Zero Trust — never trust, always verify; validate every request
Open design — security shouldn’t depend on secrecy of mechanism
Security through obscurity — why it fails as primary defense
Fail-safe / fail-secure — system fails to secure state
Due diligence — research before acting
Due care — acting responsibly with what you know

Bell-LaPadula — confidentiality focused
Rules: “No read  , no write  ”

Biba — integrity focused
Which CIA element does Biba primarily protect?
Clark-Wilson — integrity through well-formed transactions
Preventive — stops attacks before they happen
Detective — identifies attacks in progress or after
Corrective — fixes damage after attack
Deterrent — discourages attacks
Compensating — alternative when primary control isn’t feasible
Administrative / managerial — policies, procedures, training
Technical / logical — firewalls, encryption, access controls
Physical — locks, guards, cameras, fences
Kill chain — sequential steps from recon to objective
MITRE ATT&CK — knowledge base of adversary tactics and techniques

NIST Cybersecurity Framework — five core functions:
1.   2.   3.   4.   5.
Diamond Model — adversary, capability, infrastructure, victim
Passive reconnaissance — no direct interaction with target
Active reconnaissance — direct interaction, detectable
Footprinting — gathering info about target organization
Enumeration — extracting specific info (users, shares, services)
Banner grabbing — identifying service versions
OSINT (Open Source Intelligence) — publicly available info
WHOIS lookups — domain registration info
DNS reconnaissance — zone transfers, record enumeration
Google dorking — advanced search operators to find exposed info
Shodan — search engine for internet-connected devices
Metadata analysis — extracting info from document properties
Social media mining — OSINT from public profiles
CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) — standardized vulnerability IDs
Exploit-DB — database of public exploits
Attack surface — all points where attacker can try to enter

content.text: If you did the labs, you know this. If you didn’t, good luck.
Record your actual findings from Lab 1.2:
Ethics, communication, and judgment matter as much as technical skills.

items: What must you verify FIRST before conducting any reconnaissance against a target?
True or False: You may exceed your authorized scope if you discover critical vulnerabilities. Explain.
You discover exposed customer PII during an engagement. What’s the appropriate action?
What’s the difference between responsible disclosure and full disclosure?
How would you explain an outdated Apache vulnerability to a non-technical CTO?
A client can only fix 2 of 5 findings this quarter. What criteria help prioritize?
You find an outdated web server. Red team perspective: how would you exploit it? Blue team: what’s the fix?
A website reveals employee email format and leadership names. Red: how is this useful? Blue: what should change?
How can the same reconnaissance tools serve both attackers and defenders?

content.text: If you can answer these, you understand the material. If not, go back and review.

items: Explain the relationship between threats, vulnerabilities, and risks.
Why is data typically more valuable than the hardware storing it?
What makes APTs more dangerous than script kiddies? (Think: resources, patience, sophistication)
Why are insider threats harder to detect than external threats?
A bank requires two employees to authorize large transfers. What principle? Why effective?
In Bell-LaPadula, can a Secret user read Top Secret? Write to Unclassified? Why?
What is Zero Trust and why does it matter in modern networks?
What are the five NIST CSF core functions?
What’s the primary difference between a virus and a worm?
Why is security through obscurity unreliable as a primary defense?

closing.body: That’s everything. If you worked through this worksheet honestly—defining terms, answering questions, recording your lab results—you’re prepared for the Module 1 Exam and you’ve built a foundation for Security+. If you skimmed it or skipped it, that’s your choice. The exam will reveal which group you’re in.
